title: Patlıcan salatası
subtitle: Roasted and smoked aubergine salad with garlic and lemon
excerpt: Aubergines charred directly over a flame or under a very hot grill until the skin blackens and the flesh collapses, then peeled, chopped, and mixed with garlic, lemon, and olive oil. Smoky, silky, and sharp. Served as a meze or alongside grilled meats.
sourceNotes: Patlıcan salatası is a preparation found throughout Turkey and the broader Levantine region, where smoked aubergine preparations are documented from the Ottoman period. It is related to the Arabic mutabbal and baba ghanoush, and to the South Caucasian badrijani. The charring technique (cooking directly over an open flame) is documented in Turkish, Arab, and Persian cooking literature as the method for extracting the maximum smoke flavour from the aubergine's flesh. Artusi's coverage of vegetable preparations includes a similar Italian version (melanzane grigliate) in La scienza in cucina (1891, public domain).

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps refrigerated for 2 days. The smoke flavour deepens overnight.

content.text: The difference between this and baba ghanoush is small but real. Patlıcan salatası is typically lighter and cleaner: garlic, lemon, olive oil, and sometimes a spoonful of yoghurt for creaminess. The focus is the smoke from the charred skin rather than the tahini that dominates many Lebanese versions. Get the charring right and the flavour follows.

The best result comes from charring over a direct gas flame: the aubergine goes straight onto the burner, the skin burns black while the flesh steams inside, and it is done in 15 minutes. A very hot grill (broiler) takes 25 minutes and produces less smoke flavour but is a reasonable alternative. Do not use the oven at a moderate temperature — it roasts rather than chars, and the smoke flavour does not develop.

Over a gas flame: place the {{aubergine}} aubergines directly on the burner over a high flame. Cook, turning with tongs every 4 to 5 minutes, for 15 to 20 minutes until the skin is completely black and collapsed and the flesh feels very soft when pressed. Over a grill: put the aubergines on a baking tray under the grill at its highest setting, turning every 8 minutes, for about 25 minutes total. Transfer the charred aubergines to a colander set over a bowl. Cut a slit in each and leave to drain and cool for 10 minutes.

Peel off and discard all the blackened skin. Scrape any remaining bits of char away. The flesh should be grey and very soft. Chop roughly with a knife — do not blend. Place in a bowl and squeeze out any excess liquid. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic, the {{lemon}} juice, and {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil. Add the {{plain-yoghurt}} of yoghurt if using. Stir to combine. Season generously with salt. Refrigerate for 30 minutes to let the flavours develop.
Spread on a plate. Scatter the {{parsley-flat}} of parsley and sumac over if using. Drizzle with a little more olive oil. Serve with warm flatbread.

items.symptom: No smoke flavour; dish tastes of roasted rather than smoked aubergine
items.cause: Cooked in oven at moderate temperature rather than over direct flame or high grill
items.fix: Use the highest possible heat source; gas flame is ideal; the skin must fully blacken

items.symptom: Texture is watery and the salad does not hold together
items.cause: Aubergine not drained after charring; excess moisture retained
items.fix: Drain over a colander for at least 10 minutes; press gently to expel liquid; squeeze out more before mixing

items.symptom: Garlic flavour is too raw and aggressive
items.cause: Too much garlic, or garlic added without letting the salad rest
items.fix: Use only 2 small cloves; rest the salad in the fridge for 30 minutes, which mellows the raw garlic

items.symptom: Charred bits ended up in the flesh
items.cause: Skin not fully removed; or scraping too fast
items.fix: Take time to remove all black skin; rinse briefly under cold water if some char remains, then squeeze dry

content.text: Smoked aubergine preparations appear throughout the cooking of the Eastern Mediterranean and Levant — in Turkey as patlıcan salatası, in Lebanon and Syria as baba ghanoush or mutabbal, in Georgia as badrijani, in Greece as melitzanosalata. All are expressions of the same insight: that the aubergine's spongy flesh becomes something extraordinary when exposed to direct fire, absorbing the smoke from its own burning skin. The Turkish version tends to be lighter than the Lebanese, using less or no tahini and sometimes a spoonful of yoghurt instead. It is a meze constant, found at every table before the main course, and eaten with flatbread as a starter in its own right.
